LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines two `source` entries: one pointing to the main repository via `git+https://github.com/TwilitRealm/dusklight.git` with a pinned tag `v${pkgver}`, and the second a git submodule dependency from `git+https://github.com/encounter/aurora.git`. Both sources are fetched over HTTPS from GitHub, a trusted and expected origin for upstream code. The `sha256sums` array marks the first source with a valid checksum and the second (a Git VCS source) with 'SKIP' — which is standard and acceptable practice for git sources.

There is no command substitution, dynamic sourcing, or executable logic at the global scope or within `pkgver()`. The `pkgver` function is absent, so version resolution relies solely on the static `pkgver=1.4.1` assignment. No code is executed during sourcing beyond standard variable declarations. The `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` functions contain build-time operations, but they are not executed under `--noprepare --nobuild --nodeps`, so they are out of scope for this narrow gate.

Fetching the sources and parsing the PKGBUILD in this context involves only safe, declarative operations: cloning public repositories over HTTPS and defining metadata. There is no evidence of payload execution, data exfiltration, or remote code evaluation during the sourcing phase.
</details>
<summary>
No dangerous code in sourcing or source fetch phase</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a patch for a CMake configuration file (`nodConfig.cmake.in`) used in the build system of a software package. It updates internal CMake variables to use full installation paths (e.g., `@CMAKE_INSTALL_FULL_LIBDIR@` instead of constructing paths manually with `PACKAGE_PREFIX_DIR`). This is a standard and safe improvement in CMake packaging to ensure correct path resolution, especially when the package is installed in a non-default prefix.

There is no executable code, no network operations, no file manipulation outside the build system, and no evidence of obfuscation or malicious intent. The changes are purely related to build configuration and do not introduce any runtime behavior or side effects. The patch modifies only path variables used during the CMake configuration step, which is a normal part of building software.

The patch originates from a developer email and follows standard Git patch format, consistent with typical open-source development workflows. No dangerous commands or data exfiltration mechanisms are present.
</details>
<summary>
Benign CMake path correction, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

It supports three provider modes: "system", "package", and "vendor", along with an "auto" mode that selects one of these based on platform and availability.

In "vendor" mode, it downloads the SDL3 source from `https://github.com/libsdl-org/SDL`, a trusted upstream, and applies a local patch for Android/Nintendo support—this is a normal build-time operation.

There are no signs of malicious behavior such as data exfiltration, use of obfuscated commands, unauthorized network requests, or execution of untrusted code. All network destinations are relevant to the package's purpose (SDL3 integration), and the operations are consistent with standard CMake-based dependency resolution. The use of `FetchContent` and dynamic URL construction is typical in CMake projects and does not pose a supply-chain risk here, as the sources are versioned and explicitly defined.
</details>
